title: DNS Issue Event Tracking
dataSources: `index=thousandeyes_events sourcetype=cisco:thousandeyes:event` from the Event input in `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` (Splunkbase 7719). DNS Issue events are generated by ThousandEyes' Internet Insights engine when DNS resolution anomalies are detected — not from your tests alone, but correlated across the entire ThousandEyes customer base. This detects problems in third-party DNS infrastructure (e.g., a major public resolver outage, a TLD server degradation, or an authoritative DNS provider failure) that affect your monitored domains. Event fields: `type` = `DNS Issue`, `severity`, `state`, `thousandeyes.test.name`, `thousandeyes.permalink`.
app: `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` — Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk ([Splunkbase 7719](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7719)), Cisco Supported.
spl:
`event_index` type="DNS Issue"
| stats count earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen by severity, state, thousandeyes.test.name
| eval duration_min = round((last_seen - first_seen) / 60, 0)
| sort severity, -count
description: Surfaces ThousandEyes Internet Insights events specifically for DNS infrastructure anomalies, complementing the metric-based DNS monitoring in UC-5.9.13–17. While those UCs detect YOUR DNS test failures, this UC detects DNS infrastructure problems detected across the entire ThousandEyes customer base — providing early warning of third-party DNS provider outages before your individual tests show clear failure patterns.
value: Third-party DNS outages (Route 53, Cloudflare DNS, Dyn, Akamai DNS) have caused some of the most widespread internet disruptions in history — the 2016 Dyn DDoS attack took down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and hundreds of other sites. When your organization uses a third-party DNS provider, you depend on infrastructure you don't control. Internet Insights DNS Issue events give you early warning of these third-party failures, often 5–15 minutes before your individual test metrics degrade enough to trigger UC-5.9.13 alerts. This advance warning lets the DNS team prepare failover to a secondary DNS provider before the primary fails completely.
implementation: Uses the same Event input as UC-5.9.18. DNS Issue events are one of six event types fetched by the Event input. Filter by `type="DNS Issue"` to focus on DNS-specific events. Correlate with UC-5.9.13 (DNS availability metrics) to confirm whether the detected DNS issue is affecting your specific domains.
visualization: (1) Single value: active DNS Issue events (red ≥ 1). (2) Events timeline by severity. (3) Table: test name, severity, state, duration — sorted by state (active first). (4) Correlation panel: side-by-side with UC-5.9.13 DNS availability metrics for the same time window.
knownFalsePositives:
**DNS Issues for domains/providers you don't depend on.** Internet Insights may detect DNS anomalies for DNS providers used by other ThousandEyes customers but not by your organization. Distinguish by cross-referencing the affected DNS provider with your DNS infrastructure. Only alert on DNS Issues that affect tests monitoring YOUR domains.

**Transient DNS issues during provider maintenance.** DNS providers perform rolling maintenance that may trigger brief DNS Issue events. Check whether the event resolves within 15 minutes and whether your DNS availability metrics (UC-5.9.13) are unaffected.

**DNS Issue events caused by DNSSEC key rollovers.** A widespread DNSSEC key rollover (e.g., root zone KSK rollover) may trigger DNS Issue events as some resolvers temporarily fail validation. This is typically planned and communicated in advance.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• All prerequisites from UC-5.9.18 apply — Event API input configured, `event_index` macro pointing to `index=thousandeyes_events sourcetype=cisco:thousandeyes:event`.
• **ThousandEyes license includes Internet Insights.** DNS Issue events come from the Internet Insights engine. Verify in ThousandEyes: **Internet Insights → Overview** — if this page loads with data, you have access.
• **UC-5.9.13 (DNS Availability Monitoring) operational.** This UC correlates Internet Insights DNS Issue events (global, third-party DNS infrastructure) with YOUR DNS availability metrics from UC-5.9.13. Together they answer: "A global DNS problem was detected — does it affect OUR domains?"
• **DNS infrastructure inventory documented.** Know your DNS architecture before operationalizing:
  - **Authoritative DNS providers:** Who hosts your domain's DNS records? (e.g., Route 53, Cloudflare DNS, Azure DNS, internal BIND/Windows DNS).
  - **Recursive resolvers used by your clients:** What do your endpoints and servers use for DNS resolution? (e.g., Cisco Umbrella, Google 8.8.8.8, corporate DNS servers).
  - **DNS failover capability:** Do you have a secondary authoritative provider? Can you switch resolvers quickly?
• **DNS response team identified.** DNS issues cascade into total application failure. Identify the team that manages DNS and their escalation path.
• **Splunk role:** `srchIndexesAllowed` must include `thousandeyes_events`.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
DNS Issue events are fetched by the same Event API input configured in UC-5.9.18. No separate input is needed.

Verify DNS Issue events are present (infrequent — search 30+ days):
```spl
index=thousandeyes_events sourcetype="cisco:thousandeyes:event" type="DNS Issue" earliest=-90d
| stats count by severity, state
```
If this returns 0 over 90 days, DNS issues simply haven't been detected. Verify the Event input works by checking for other event types:
```spl
index=thousandeyes_events sourcetype="cisco:thousandeyes:event" earliest=-7d
| stats count by type
```

**Understanding DNS Issue events vs DNS metrics:**
- **DNS Issue events (this UC):** Generated by Internet Insights from AGGREGATE analysis across the ThousandEyes customer base. Detects WIDESPREAD DNS infrastructure problems (DNS provider outages, root server issues, DNSSEC failures). These are third-party infrastructure problems you don't control.
- **DNS metrics (UC-5.9.13/5.9.14):** Generated by YOUR DNS Server and DNS Trace tests. Detects DNS resolution problems specific to YOUR domains and YOUR resolvers.
- **Together:** DNS Issue event fires (global problem detected) + your DNS availability drops (your domains affected) = confirmed impact requiring immediate action. DNS Issue event fires + your DNS is fine = third-party problem doesn't affect you.

**DNS Issue event fields:**
- `type` — "DNS Issue".
- `severity` — "info", "warning", "minor", "major", "critical". Reflects the scale of the DNS disruption.
- `state` — "active" or "cleared".
- `description` — text describing the DNS issue (affected provider, infrastructure, estimated scope).
- `thousandeyes.test.name` — your tests affected by this issue (may be empty for global events detected from aggregate data).
- `thousandeyes.permalink` — link to the full event view in ThousandEyes UI.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
**DNS Issue event summary:**
```spl
`event_index` type="DNS Issue"
| stats count earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen by severity, state, thousandeyes.test.name, description
| eval duration_min=round((last_seen - first_seen) / 60, 0)
| eval first_seen_human=strftime(first_seen, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"), last_seen_human=strftime(last_seen, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")
| table severity, state, description, thousandeyes.test.name, first_seen_human, last_seen_human, duration_min, count
| sort severity, -count
```

**Understanding this SPL**

Same structure as UC-5.9.18 (Network Outage) but filtered to `type="DNS Issue"`. The `description` field is particularly important for DNS Issues because it identifies WHICH DNS provider or infrastructure is affected.

**Active DNS Issue alert (real-time):**
```spl
`event_index` type="DNS Issue" state="active" (severity="major" OR severity="critical")
| dedup description
| table _time, severity, description, thousandeyes.test.name, thousandeyes.permalink
| sort -_time
```
Filtering to major/critical avoids noise from minor DNS hiccups. Dedup by description ensures one alert per unique DNS issue.

**Correlation with YOUR DNS availability metrics (the key differentiator for this UC):**
```spl
`event_index` type="DNS Issue" state="active" earliest=-4h
| eval data_source="Internet_Insights"
| table _time, data_source, severity, description, thousandeyes.permalink
| append [
  search `stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="dns-server" earliest=-4h
  | stats avg(dns.lookup.availability) as dns_avail by dns.question.name
  | where dns_avail < 100
  | eval data_source="Your_DNS_Metrics", severity="measured"
  | table _time, data_source, severity, dns.question.name, dns_avail
]
| sort -_time
```
This combined view shows Internet Insights events (global DNS problem detected) alongside your own DNS metric failures (your domains affected). If both appear simultaneously, confirmed impact. If only Internet Insights shows an issue but your DNS metrics are fine, the global problem doesn't affect you.

**DNS Issue trending (pattern analysis over 90 days):**
```spl
`event_index` type="DNS Issue" earliest=-90d
| stats count earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen by severity, description
| eval duration_min=round((last_seen - first_seen) / 60, 0)
| sort -first_seen
```
Review quarterly to identify recurring DNS infrastructure issues that may warrant architecture changes (secondary DNS provider, different resolvers).

**Scheduling:** cron `*/5 * * * *`, time range `-15m to now`. DNS failures cascade rapidly — every application that requires DNS resolution (i.e., ALL of them) breaks. Five-minute detection is essential.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Confirm Event input is active.** Verify ANY event type is flowing: `index=thousandeyes_events | stats count by type`. If data exists for other event types, the input works and DNS Issue events will appear when detected.

(b) **Understand event frequency.** DNS Issue events are infrequent — they represent MAJOR DNS infrastructure failures visible across the ThousandEyes customer base. Weeks or months without an event is normal. Don't assume pipeline failure.

(c) **ThousandEyes UI cross-reference.** Check **Internet Insights → DNS** in the ThousandEyes UI. If DNS issues are shown there but not in Splunk, the Event API input may lag or filter them.

(d) **Correlation validation.** If a DNS Issue event appears, immediately check your DNS metrics from UC-5.9.13: `\`stream_index\` thousandeyes.test.type="dns-server" | stats avg(dns.lookup.availability) as avail by dns.question.name`. If your domains show < 100% availability simultaneously, the correlation confirms impact.

(e) **Alert routing test.** Use a Network Outage event (more frequent) to test alert routing, then switch the filter to `type="DNS Issue"` for production.

Step 4 — Operationalize
**Dashboard** (integrate into the UC-5.9.13 "DNS Health" dashboard as an additional row):
• Row (add to existing dashboard) — Internet Insights DNS Issues: panel showing active DNS Issue events with severity, description, and ThousandEyes permalink. When empty (normal), shows "No active DNS infrastructure issues." When populated, provides context for why YOUR DNS metrics may be degrading.

**Alerting:**
• Any `state="active"` DNS Issue with severity ≥ "major" → immediate high-urgency notification to DNS team / `#dns-ops`.
• DNS Issue event AND your DNS availability (UC-5.9.13) drops below 100% simultaneously → CRITICAL notification. Confirmed impact on your domains.

**Runbook** (owner: DNS team / infrastructure):
1. **DNS Issue event detected — determine impact.** (a) Open ThousandEyes permalink to identify the affected DNS infrastructure (provider, servers, geographic scope). (b) Check UC-5.9.13: are YOUR DNS availability metrics also degraded? If no → monitor but don't escalate. If yes → confirmed impact, proceed to step 2.
2. **Impact confirmed — immediate DNS failover.** (a) If you have a secondary authoritative DNS provider, initiate failover: update NS delegation or activate GSLB policies. (b) If the issue affects recursive resolvers (e.g., Cisco Umbrella outage), switch corporate resolver settings to backup (e.g., 8.8.8.8, 1.1.1.1). (c) Flush DNS caches on affected servers: `ipconfig /flushdns` (Windows), `systemd-resolve --flush-caches` (Linux).
3. **Communicate to users.** "DNS infrastructure issues are affecting application access. The team has activated backup DNS. Some applications may be slow to respond. Status updates will follow."
4. **Post-incident.** (a) Document timeline and impact. (b) Review whether your DNS architecture needs redundancy improvements (multi-provider authoritative, multi-resolver recursive). (c) If your DNS provider had an SLA, evaluate whether the outage warrants an SLA credit.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **No DNS Issue events appear (ever)** — Likely normal. Verify: (a) Internet Insights is licensed. (b) Event input is working for other types. (c) Major DNS outages are rare (1–2 per quarter globally).

• **DNS Issue event but your DNS metrics are completely unaffected** — The affected DNS infrastructure may not be in your resolution path. This is expected. Consider filtering alerts to only fire when BOTH an Internet Insights event AND your DNS metrics degrade.

• **Too many low-severity DNS Issue events** — Filter the alert to `severity="major" OR severity="critical"` to reduce noise from minor DNS fluctuations.

• **DNS metrics degrade but no Internet Insights event** — The DNS problem may be specific to YOUR domains or YOUR resolvers, not a global issue. Internet Insights only fires for WIDESPREAD problems. Use UC-5.9.13/5.9.14 for YOUR DNS health monitoring.

• **All common troubleshooting** — See UC-5.9.18 Step 5 for Event input configuration, OAuth, and macro setup issues.
grandmaExplanation: We get an alert when the internet's phone book services are having trouble, so we can switch to a backup phone book before our customers can't find us anymore.
